headlines.text: Trump orders Navy to shoot Iranian boats laying mines in Strait of Hormuz
Iran seizes two cargo ships, fires on third — calls US blockade an 'act of war'
Senate defeats Iran War Powers resolution for a fifth time, 55-46
Pentagon fires Navy Secretary John Phelan after clashes with Hegseth
Trump approval rating sinks to mid-30s; aides draft midterm strategy to avoid a referendum on him
Senate Republicans pass ICE/border patrol budget blueprint without Democratic support amid partial DHS shutdown
Michael Jackson biopic 'Michael' eyes record $150M global opening this weekend despite 33% on Rotten Tomatoes
S&P 500 and Nasdaq closed at record highs Wednesday; stocks waver Thursday on Iran stalemate and software earnings miss
National average gas price hits $4.03 a gallon — experts say sub-$3 gas is months away at minimum
UN warns Iran war has pushed 32 million people toward poverty; Strait of Hormuz still closed on Day 55

breaking.text: Trump orders Navy to 'shoot and kill' Iranian boats placing mines in Strait of Hormuz as Iran seizes two ships and fires on third

stories.hed: Trump Says 'Shoot on Sight' While Iran Runs a Toll Booth at Hormuz
stories.dek: Trump extended the ceasefire indefinitely, then immediately ordered the Navy to fire on any Iranian boat laying mines — because nothing says 'peace talks' like a shoot-on-sight order. Meanwhile Iran is literally charging ships a toll to pass through the strait and pocketing up to $20 million a day, which is the most aggressive valet service in maritime history.

stories.hed: Lebanon Ceasefire Frays as Israel Kills Five Including Journalist Amal Khalil
stories.dek: Israel killed at least five people in southern Lebanon despite the 10-day US-brokered ceasefire, including Al-Akhbar correspondent Amal Khalil, who had previously received death threats via WhatsApp from an Israeli phone number. A second round of Israel-Lebanon ambassador-level talks is underway in Washington today.

stories.hed: Pentagon Fires Navy Secretary Phelan After He Suggested Outsourcing Warship Production Abroad
stories.dek: Navy Secretary John Phelan was abruptly fired after signaling openness to building US Navy warships overseas — a comment that somehow surprised an administration actively fighting a naval war. Defense Secretary Hegseth had also reportedly been frustrated with Phelan's pace on domestic shipbuilding reform, making this the 34th senior official removed under Trump.

stories.hed: Trump's Approval Sinks to Mid-30s; Aides Plot Midterm Strategy to Make It About Democrats Instead
stories.dek: Three polls this week placed Trump's approval in the mid-30 percent range, so advisers are developing a midterm strategy that frames 2026 as a choice between the two parties rather than a verdict on Trump — a classic move that roughly translates to 'please don't look at us, look at them.'

stories.hed: Mysterious Traders Made Millions on Suspiciously Well-Timed Bets Tied to the Iran War
stories.dek: Investigators are looking into a cluster of traders who made extraordinary profits on options and energy contracts placed just before key Iran war escalations — the kind of timing that makes you wonder whether someone got the memo early, or just has incredibly good vibes about global conflict.

markets.context: Stocks wavered as Iran-US peace talks stalled and IBM plus ServiceNow cratered on earnings, while oil surged for a fourth straight day on Hormuz tension.

gas.note: Gas is $4 a gallon and the Strait of Hormuz is still closed — congrats, your commute is now a geopolitical event.

popCulture.hed: Michael Jackson Biopic 'Michael' Opens Tomorrow Eyeing $150M Global Debut Despite 33% on Rotten Tomatoes
popCulture.dek: The Antoine Fuqua-directed biopic starring MJ's real nephew Jaafar Jackson is tracking for the biggest opening ever for a music biopic — critics hate it, fans don't care, and the internet has spent the week debating whether skipping the abuse allegations makes it a hagiography or just a very expensive music video.

genZ.definition: A satirical TikTok phrase where users tag 'Grok, is this true?' under viral posts — originally a sincere question directed at Elon Musk's X chatbot, it has evolved into ironic shorthand for mocking obvious lies, political spin, or absurd news. In April 2026 it has exploded as a format for reacting to war coverage and political claims.
genZ.usage: Trump says the naval blockade is 'basically a ceasefire.' Grok, is this true?

tldr: On Day 55 of the Iran war, Trump ordered the Navy to shoot Iranian mine-layers while simultaneously extending the ceasefire indefinitely, which is the diplomatic equivalent of hugging someone and punching them at the same time. Gas is $4 a gallon, the stock market is shrugging, and the Michael Jackson biopic opens tomorrow to rave reviews from exactly zero critics and roughly 100 million fans who already bought tickets.
